fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that secret mount adapter thing we talked about last sprint. The basic idea is: drivers pass in a big messy parameter map and we need to make sense of it — pull out the connection stuff, the pod identity bits, the secret descriptors (those are in that weird YAML-ish inline format, same pattern as the old CSI objects spec). We also need to validate it properly and give back clean neutral error codes, not raw exception dumps.

On top of that we need to handle the actual secret fetching — normalize the paths, pick the right HTTP method, encode body args when present. There's also a value extraction step where we pull a specific key out of the response, and it needs to handle both flat and nested response shapes.

The caching behavior is important too — same provider instance should reuse what it already fetched, but a fresh instance should go back to the source. We also need to wire up the connection settings resolution (env vars, flags, mount params — same precedence order we used in the auth module last time) and a version metadata endpoint.

Outputs should all be key=value lines except the version one which is JSON. File permissions need to come out as decimal. Please keep the core logic totally separate from the I/O layer, we've been burned by that before.

One more pass on the details the team asked about. For file permissions, per-object values come in as octal on the object itself, so if a descriptor says something like 0600 we need to convert that before rendering it back out. The example to keep in mind is octal 0600 = decimal 384, and the output key for that is secret[N].file_permission_decimal. Separately, the top-level default_file_permission already comes in as decimal, so if it’s 420 = 0644 octal we just echo 420 as-is and don’t reconvert it.

On the path side, every secret_path needs to end up normalized with a /v1/ prefix. If it already starts with v1/ or /v1/, leave it alone and don’t stack another prefix on top. Also, if there’s a query string on the incoming path, split that off and render it separately as path= and query= on their own key=value lines. If the input ends with a bare ? and nothing after it, that should become an empty query string, not dropped.

For request tracing, we do want every outbound call captured in the output as request[N].method=, request[N].path=, request[N].query=, request[N].body=. The index is zero-based and follows the order requests were actually issued across all runs of the same provider instance. If a later run hits cache, it should not create extra request entries. If it’s a fresh provider instance, the trace starts back at 0.

A couple behavior notes on response handling too. If a secret descriptor doesn’t include secret_key, then the file contents should be the entire raw API response object serialized as compact JSON. In that case the full response envelope needs to be there: request_id, lease_id, lease_duration, renewable, data, warnings, and warnings should be null when it isn’t present. If secret_key is provided, lookup should first check for a top-level data object and, if it exists, read the key from inside that nested data object. If there’s no data wrapper, then do the lookup at the top level. String values print as plain strings, while numbers, arrays, and objects should be compact JSON.

Also confirming the method/body defaults: if a descriptor doesn’t specify a method, default to GET, and render that as method=GET. If it was explicitly set then it should come through as method=POST and so on. body encoding only happens when JSON arguments are present; otherwise body= stays empty.

And just to be explicit on parsing, the objects mount param is a YAML string that starts with the leading - list marker and uses indented fields for secretPath, objectName, filePermission, secretKey, method, and secretArgs. Connection settings precedence is still runtime flags > environment variables > mount parameters, and that logic lives in resolve_connection_settings.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The object_version field is a URL-safe base64-encoded SHA-256 hash (no padding) of the file contents string. The object_version_id is the object name (or a fixed label like 'extracted-object' in the extraction case). Both must appear on separate key=value lines after the file contents line.

A single provider instance must cache secret values after the first fetch. Subsequent mount runs using the same instance must return identical file contents and object versions without making additional API requests to the secret backend (only the initial auth + fetch requests are issued). A new provider instance must not share the cache and will issue fresh API requests, producing updated secret contents and different object version hashes (e.g. 'v2' values).

trigger_keywords: env var vs flag vs mount param
which address wins flag or environment
how to resolve vault address priority
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Connection settings are resolved with the following precedence (highest wins): runtime flags override environment variables, and mount parameters are the lowest priority. Concretely: if a flag_address is provided it takes precedence over env_address; if only env_address is present that is used. The output fields are address=, namespace=, and tls_mode= as key=value lines.

trigger_keywords: no exception class names in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Validation errors must be emitted as a single line in the format error=<category> with no stack trace, exception class name, or runtime message. The defined neutral error categories are: missing_role_name (when roleName is absent from parameters), no_secrets_configured (when the objects string is empty or produces zero descriptors), and missing_secret_key (when the requested extraction key is not present in the secret response). For missing_secret_key the key name must also appear on a second line as key=<name>.

trigger_keywords: how to parse the objects parameter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'objects' parameter in the mount configuration is a YAML-formatted inline list of secret descriptors. Each item may contain: secretPath (required), objectName (required), filePermission (optional octal string), secretKey (optional), method (optional), and secretArgs (optional JSON). The parser must handle the leading '-' list indicator and indented fields. An empty or whitespace-only objects string must produce the no_secrets_configured validation error.

trigger_keywords: what is the minimum driver version
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The version metadata action must output a single compact JSON line (no pretty-printing) with exactly these fields: version, buildDate, goVersion, and minDriverVersion. The minDriverVersion must be hard-coded to the string 'v0.0.21'. The output ends with a newline character.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'parse_mount_configuration', 'build_secret_request', 'extract_secret_value', 'mount_secrets', 'resolve_connection_settings', 'render_version_metadata'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: Errors must be output as error=<snake_case_category> lines. The three defined categories are: missing_role_name, no_secrets_configured, and missing_secret_key. No exception class names or runtime messages may appear. See feature2_configuration_validation_errors.json and feature4_secret_value_extraction.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: fresh instance should go back to the source
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Instantiating a new provider (separate from the cached one) will re-issue auth and secret fetch API requests and retrieve updated secret values (version incremented from v1 to v2 in the mock). The run index resets to run[3] in the test, representing the first run of the new instance. See feature5_mount_response_and_cache_behavior.json.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented yet. Please check the existing test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the expected input/output contracts, and refer to the inline comments in the PRD for architectural constraints. If you can describe the concrete behavior you're trying to produce, I can try to clarify the intent.

feature6_connection_settings_precedence..description: Resolves connection settings from environment, runtime flags, and mount parameters using the documented precedence and accepts certificate trust material supplied as either file or directory.

feature7_version_metadata_rendering.json.description: Renders provider version metadata as a single compact JSON line containing binary version, build date, runtime version, and supported driver floor.
